summary: Celebrate Life was our 50th anniversary CD, a significant milestone for us. It marked a period where we had slowed down recording albums but wanted to commemorate our long journey together. This project was a joyful reflection of our music and history as a band.

Rickey Wasson is a key figure in our recent recording projects. He runs the studio in Clay City where we recorded our latest album and also manages Meadowgreen Music Park. Rickey is incredibly respectful and knowledgeable about our music, bringing expertise and care to the production process, and he’s been a great collaborator and support throughout.

Clay City is where Rickey Wasson's studio is located, and it’s where we gathered to record our latest album. It’s conveniently close for all of us coming from different places, making it a practical and comfortable spot for our sessions. The community there supports the music scene, especially through venues like Meadowgreen Music Park.

Meadowgreen Music Park is a vibrant place run by Rickey Wasson that honors both past and current music traditions. It’s a hub for the next generation of musicians and keeps the spirit of our musical heritage alive. Although I haven’t been there yet, I know it’s where our upcoming album release will be held, and it’s an exciting venue for us.

Chuck Cox is a good friend and a talented musician who has been helping with research on our project. He’s been encouraging me to visit Meadowgreen Music Park, which I haven’t had the chance to do yet. Chuck’s involvement adds depth to our work, especially in connecting us with the broader musical community.

Raymond McLain is a central member of our band and often takes the lead in our production efforts. He’s part of the team that selects songs and shapes our albums, contributing original compositions like 'Dance in Old Kentucky' and 'River City Blues.' His leadership and musicianship are vital to our sound and success.

Alice McLain is part of our band and contributes both musically and personally. She sings on songs like 'Homegrown Tomatoes,' which was a fun studio piece inspired by our shared gardening hobby. Alice and I both grow tomatoes, and bringing them to the studio added a special, lighthearted touch to our sessions.

Al McLain is a member of our band and part of the core group that decides on songs and rehearses for our albums. He works closely with Raymond, Alice, and me to prepare material for recording and performances. Al’s contributions help keep our music cohesive and vibrant.

Daxson McLain is part of our family band and joins us in rehearsals and recording sessions. Like the rest of us, he balances a busy schedule but is committed to making the music come together. His participation strengthens the family dynamic and musical harmony we share.

Brink Brinkman is a friend who contributed a song called 'Would I' to our latest album. It’s exciting to include a piece from Brink, adding fresh material to our collection. His generosity and creativity enrich our project and broaden our musical palette.

The McLain Family Band is our musical group, consisting of family members who have played together for decades. We self-produce our albums and have a deep respect for each other’s roles in the band. Our music includes both original songs and traditional tunes, and recording together is always a joyful experience.

Buck White wrote the tune for 'Down Home Waltz,' a song on our new album where our father wrote the words. His contribution is meaningful to us, blending his composition with our family’s lyrics. Buck’s music has been an important part of our repertoire and legacy.

Our Aunt Rosemary wrote the song 'Blow the Candle Out,' which we included on our latest album. Her songwriting adds a special family connection to our music. Including her work honors her contribution and keeps her spirit alive in our performances.

'Kentucky Moon' is a brand new song that Raymond and I wrote recently for this album project. It’s fresh material created specifically to fit the theme and feel of our current work. Writing and recording it has been an exciting part of the process.

Asa Martin was a friend and a musician with a rich history going back to the vaudeville days. We recorded his song 'Down in Old Kentucky' on our new album, which connects us to that musical heritage. His legacy is important to us and to the tradition we carry forward.

'Dance in Old Kentucky' is a song written by Raymond that we have performed before but never recorded until now. It’s a fun piece that fits well with our style and the themes of our new album. Recording it was a fresh experience for the band.

'Down Home Waltz' is a sweet song on our album with music by Buck White and lyrics by our father. It holds a special place in our repertoire because of that family collaboration. The song captures a heartfelt, traditional feel that resonates with our audience.

'Blow the Candle Out' was written by our Aunt Rosemary and is included on our latest album. It’s a meaningful family song that we cherish performing and recording. Her songwriting adds a personal touch to our music collection.

'A Mountain of Memories' is another brand new song that Raymond and I wrote for this project. It’s fresh material that reflects our current creative direction. Including it on the album adds depth and personal meaning to the collection.

'Down in Old Kentucky' is a song by Asa Martin that we recorded for our new album. It connects us to a rich musical past and honors a dear friend’s legacy. Recording this song was a way to celebrate traditional Kentucky music within our family band.

'River City Blues' is an instrumental piece written by Raymond that we included in our new album. It’s a fun and exciting tune that showcases our musical skills and adds variety to the collection. Playing and recording it was a joyful experience.

'Homegrown Tomatoes' is a special studio song sung by Alice that we recorded while bringing fresh tomatoes to each session. It was a lighthearted and unique part of the recording process, inspired by our gardening hobby. The song adds a personal and fun element to the album.

Jody Miller is working on the design for our new album, helping with the visual presentation. She’s a great collaborator and brings creativity to the project’s packaging. Her involvement ensures the album looks as good as it sounds.

Jesse Wells is a musician touring with Tyler Childers who shared insights about the popularity of vinyl records. He mentioned that for Tyler Childers, vinyl sales far exceed CDs, reflecting a shift in how people listen to music. His perspective helped us understand current trends among younger audiences.

Tyler Childers is a contemporary musician whose audience prefers vinyl records over CDs, according to Jesse Wells. This trend shows how vinyl is making a comeback, especially among younger listeners. It’s interesting to see how this influences music consumption today.

At Morehead State University, our students have shown a strong interest in vinyl records, with many owning turntables. This reflects a broader cultural shift toward valuing the physical album experience. It’s encouraging to see younger generations appreciating music in this traditional format.

Hindman is a place significant to our family history, where many important events occurred. We discussed our time there up to 1968, including a memorable television show and a tragic house fire on New Year's Eve. Hindman holds many memories that shaped our lives and music.

New Year's Eve 1968 was a pivotal and tragic day for our family due to a house fire in Hindman. It was a moment that deeply affected us and marked a turning point in our lives. The event is remembered with both sorrow and reflection on how it changed our path.

The year 1968 was significant for us, especially because of the house fire on New Year's Eve in Hindman. It was a difficult time that influenced our family and our music journey. Reflecting on that year helps us understand the challenges we overcame together.

Raymond Kane McLain was our father and served as music director at Berea College. He was deeply involved in our musical upbringing and contributed lyrics to songs like 'Down Home Waltz.' His leadership and musical influence have been foundational to our family band.

Berea College is where our father, Raymond Kane McLain, worked as music director. It was an important place in our family’s musical life and history. The college’s music programs and community influenced our development as musicians.

The Berea Christmas Country Dance School was an event connected to our family and musical activities, likely during our father’s tenure at Berea College. It was part of the vibrant cultural and musical traditions we were involved in. These events helped foster community and keep traditional music alive.

Bicky McClain is a family member connected to our musical heritage, though not much detail was provided in this session. Like other family members, Bicky’s presence is part of the broader story of our band and its roots. Family ties are important in our music and history.

Hillside is a place mentioned in our family history and musical journey, though details were limited in this conversation. It is part of the landscape that shaped our experiences and music. Places like Hillside contribute to the sense of home and tradition in our work.
